content.problemStatement:
If you are not familiar with graph theory terminology, please visit the end of the problem to see definitions.

You are in the graph construction business. A client of yours has asked for a special graph. A graph is called special if it satisfies the following properties:


It has ≤105\le 10^5≤105 vertices.
It is a simple, undirected, connected 3-regular graph.
It has exactly kkk bridge edges, (kkk given as input).
For a graph GGG, define f(G)f(G)f(G) to be the minimum number of edges to be removed from it to make it bipartite. The client doesn't like graphs with a high value of fff, so he asks you to minimize it.

If there doesn't exist any special graph, print −1-1−1. Otherwise, find a special graph GGG with the minimum possible value of f(G)f(G)f(G) and also find a subset of its edges of size f(G)f(G)f(G) whose removal makes it bipartite . In case there are multiple such graphs, you can output any of those.
content.inputFormat: The only line of the input contains a single integer kkk, the required number of bridge edges.
content.outputFormat:
If there are no special graphs, print a single line containing the integer −1-1−1. Otherwise, let GGG be your graph with nnn vertices numbered 1,2,…n1, 2, \ldots n1,2,…n and mmm edges numbered 1,2,…m1, 2, \ldots m1,2,…m. Let XXX be a subset of edges of size f(G)f(G)f(G) whose removal makes the graph bipartite. If there are multiple choices for XXX, you can choose any one of them.


In the first line, print nnn and mmm.
In ithi^{\text{th}}ith of the next mmm lines, print the edge numbered iii as its endpoints separated by a space.
In the last line, print f(G)f(G)f(G) followed by the indices of the edges in XXX. You can print the indices in any order.
For example if f(G)=4f(G) = 4f(G)=4, and removing edges with indices {2,4,5,7}\{2, 4, 5, 7\}{2,4,5,7} makes the graph bipartite, you can print `4 5 2 7 4`.

Your answer is considered correct if and only if it is a special graph and of all the special graphs, it has the minimum possible fff value, and removing edges in XXX, the remaining graph is bipartite.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The graph printed is a bipartite graph with 3 nodes on both sides. Each node is connected to every node on the opposite side. Every node has a degree 333; there is no bridge edge. Since it is a bipartite graph, it has a fff value of 000, which is the minimum possible.
If you already know the graph theory terms used in the statement, you can skip  reading the
following definitions:
Connected graph - A graph is said to be connected if, for every two nodes, you can reach one from the other using the edges of the graph.
Simple graph - A graph GGG is called simple if there are no self-loops (edge from a node to itself) or multiple edges( more than one edge between a pair of vertices).
3-regular graph -  A 3-regular graph is one in which every vertex is an endpoint of exactly 333 edges.
Bipartite graph - A bipartite graph is a graph whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets UUU and VVV such that every edge connects a vertex in UUU to one in VVV.
Bridge edge - An edge is called a bridge edge if on removing it, the graph is no longer connected.
